topic.what: A comprehensive narrative review of curcumin's therapeutic role across the spectrum of digestive diseases including gastritis, peptic ulcer disease, H. pylori infection, inflammatory bowel disease, and colorectal cancer, with explicit discussion of the bioavailability limitations that constrain clinical translation.
topic.how: Systematic synthesis of pre-clinical (in-vitro and animal) and clinical evidence for curcumin in GI diseases; mechanisms reviewed include NF-kB inhibition, COX-2 suppression, ROS scavenging, and direct anti-H. pylori activity; bioavailability strategies (piperine, nanoparticles, phospholipid complexes) summarised.
topic.why_matters: Curcumin is one of the most studied natural compounds for GI disease; a single structured review across disease areas provides clinicians and researchers with an actionable evidence map and highlights where the evidence is strong vs. speculative.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number and variety of digestive conditions with supporting curcumin evidence reviewed
characteristics.value: Covers gastritis, PUD, H. pylori, IBD (UC and CD), and colorectal cancer
characteristics.evidence: Narrative synthesis of published literature up to 2013
characteristics.context: Review article; depth varies by disease area

characteristics.what_it_measures: Characterisation of curcumin's oral bioavailability limitations and enhancement strategies
characteristics.value: Poor native oral bioavailability identified as major translational barrier; enhancement strategies described
characteristics.context: Critical consideration for all clinical applications

characteristics.what_it_measures: Quality of evidence for curcumin against H. pylori specifically
characteristics.value: Mostly pre-clinical; limited clinical trial data as of 2013
characteristics.evidence: In-vitro bacteriostatic/bactericidal studies and one or two small RCTs
characteristics.context: Evidence base has grown since 2013 publication

good_for: Researchers seeking an overview of curcumin's GI evidence base across multiple diseases
Identifying which GI applications have the strongest translational support
Understanding bioavailability barriers before designing curcumin clinical trials
Hypothesis generation for curcumin combination strategies in GI oncology
not_for: Current systematic meta-analysis (published 2013; evidence has evolved)
Specific dosing recommendations (bioavailability variability too high for universal dosing)
requires: Updated search to capture post-2013 RCTs and nanoformulation studies
Disease-specific evidence appraisal for any given indication

novelty: First broad cross-disease review in World Journal of Gastroenterology synthesising curcumin's full GI evidence spectrum
Explicitly frames bioavailability as the dominant translational obstacle, setting the agenda for subsequent formulation research
Provides a mechanistic map linking curcumin's molecular targets (NF-kB, COX-2, ROS) to each GI disease phenotype
open_problems: Optimal bioavailability-enhanced formulation for each GI indication (gastric vs. colonic delivery differ)
Long-term safety of enhanced-bioavailability curcumin formulations
Whether curcumin's anti-inflammatory effects in IBD are disease-modifying or merely symptomatic
Role of curcumin in chemoprevention of H. pylori-associated gastric cancer
